mnemonics.title: Lossless-Join Test
mnemonics.content: COMMON KEY: The intersection must determine something - think 'Common Key Creates Complete Relation' (CKCCR)

mnemonics.title: BCNF Definition
mnemonics.content: DETERMINANT = SUPERKEY: Every determinant (left side of FD) must be a superkey - 'D Equals S' (DES)

mnemonics.title: 3NF Definition
mnemonics.content: KEY OR PRIME: Each non-key attribute depends on key, entirely on key, or on another key attribute - '3NF Means Full Function' (TFMF)

mnemonics.title: Anomalies Types
mnemonics.content: UID: Update, Insertion, Deletion - think 'Ugly Information Damages Data' (UIDD)

mnemonics.title: Normal Form Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: BCNF > 3NF > 2NF > 1NF: Think 'Boys Can Find Nurturing Fathers' (BCFNF)

acronyms.usage: Most restrictive normal form where every determinant must be a superkey
Constraint where one attribute uniquely determines another
Normal form that eliminates transitive dependencies while preserving dependencies
Minimal superkey - minimum set of attributes that uniquely identify tuples
Minimal equivalent set of functional dependencies

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table with rows for each decomposed relation and columns for all attributes. Mark 'a' for attributes in each relation's schema and 'b' for others. Apply FDs to change values until row becomes all 'a's. If achieved, decomposition is lossless.

Create a diagram showing original FDs as arrows, then show projected FDs on each decomposed relation. Check if you can derive all original FDs from the union of projected FDs using Armstrong's axioms.
Pyramid diagram: BCNF at top (most restrictive), then 3NF, then 2NF, then 1NF at base. Each level includes all below it.

analogies.analogy: Like separating a puzzle into pieces - lossless means you can perfectly reconstruct the original puzzle without any extra or missing pieces when you join them back.
Like translating a document into another language - dependency preservation means all the original rules and meanings can still be enforced in the new structure without losing any constraints.
BCNF is like having every employee report directly to the CEO (strict hierarchy), while 3NF allows middle managers who have their own teams (more flexible but still organized).
Like writing the same address on every letter in a mailing list instead of having one address database - causes inconsistency when one copy changes.

Think of a shared document where multiple people have copies - update anomaly is when one person updates their copy but others don't see it; deletion anomaly is when removing one item accidentally removes related information.
